parts.content: Draw the Lewis (electron dot) structure of $\text{SF}_6$, showing all bonding pairs and any lone pairs.
parts.markscheme: Lewis structure showing central sulfur atom with six single bonds to fluorine atoms and three lone pairs on each fluorine atom. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Count total valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw any Lewis structure, we first determine the total number of valence electrons available. 

*   **Sulfur (S):** Group 16, so it has 6 valence electrons.
*   **Fluorine (F):** Group 17, so each has 7 valence electrons.

Since there are six fluorine atoms, the total count is:

$$6 + (6 \times 7) = 48\text{ valence electrons}$$

highlights.text: S=6, F=7x6, total=48

steps.title: Draw the skeletal structure
steps.content:
Place Sulfur as the central atom and connect it to the six Fluorine atoms using single bonds. 

Each single bond represents 2 shared electrons. Since we have 6 bonds:

$$\text{Electrons used} = 6 \text{ bonds} \times 2 = 12 \text{ electrons}$$

Subtract these from the total to find the remaining electrons:

$$48 - 12 = 36 \text{ electrons remaining}$$

highlights.text: draw six S-F single bonds

steps.title: Distribute remaining lone pairs
steps.content:
Use the remaining 36 electrons to complete the octets of the outer Fluorine atoms. Each Fluorine atom already has 2 electrons from its bond with Sulfur, so it needs 6 more (3 lone pairs) to reach a stable octet.

$$\frac{36 \text{ electrons}}{6 \text{ Fluorine atoms}} = 6 \text{ electrons per Fluorine (3 lone pairs)}$$

This uses up all 36 remaining electrons precisely.

highlights.text: distribute the remaining 36 electrons as lone pairs to complete the octets of all fluorine atoms

steps.title: Final Lewis structure check
steps.content:
The final structure shows a central Sulfur atom with **six single bonds** radiating out to six Fluorine atoms. Each Fluorine atom must be surrounded by **three lone pairs** of electrons (6 dots).

Note that Sulfur in $\text{SF}_6$ has 12 electrons in its valence shell. This is an example of an **expanded octet**, which is common for period 3 elements and below, as discussed in **Topic S2.2.13** of the IB syllabus.

highlights.text: Lewis structure showing central sulfur atom with six single bonds to fluorine atoms and three lone pairs on each fluorine atom

steps.title: Identify Sulfur's valence electrons
steps.content:
To start our **Group-Based Bonding Approach**, we identify the central atom, Sulfur ($S$). Sulfur is located in **Group 16** of the periodic table. 

Because it is in Group 16, it has $6$ valence electrons available for bonding.

highlights.text: SF_6

steps.title: Sulfur's expanded octet
steps.content:
In $\text{SF}_6$, Sulfur is bonded to six Fluorine atoms. Since Sulfur is in Period 3, it can have an **expanded octet** (as discussed in **Topic S2.2.13**). 

To accommodate six Fluorine atoms, Sulfur uses all **6 of its valence electrons** to form six separate single covalent bonds. This results in $12$ electrons around the central Sulfur atom.

steps.title: Analyze Fluorine's requirements
steps.content:
Fluorine ($F$) is in **Group 17**, meaning it has $7$ valence electrons. To reach a stable octet (8 electrons), each Fluorine atom needs to form exactly **one bond**.

After forming one bond with Sulfur, each Fluorine atom will have $6$ remaining valence electrons. These are represented as **3 lone pairs** per Fluorine atom.

steps.title: Construct the Lewis structure
steps.content:
Now we assemble the structure:
1. Place $S$ in the center.
2. Draw **six single bonds** connecting $S$ to six $F$ atoms.
3. Add **three lone pairs** (represented as dots) around every $F$ atom to ensure they each have a full octet.

$$\text{Total valence electrons} = 6 + (6 \times 7) = 48$$

In the structure, we have 6 bonds ($12\,e^-$) and 18 lone pairs ($36\,e^-$), which totals 48 electrons, confirming our structure is correct.

highlights.text: six single bonds to fluorine atoms and three lone pairs on each fluorine atom

parts.content: Predict the molecular geometry of $\text{SF}_6$ using VSEPR theory.
parts.markscheme: Octahedral geometry (6 bonding pairs around central atom) <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To start with the **Electron Domain Counting** approach, we first look at the central atom, sulfur ($S$). Sulfur is in **Group 16** of the periodic table, which means it has 6 valence electrons.

This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**. Knowing the group number helps you quickly identify the number of electrons available for bonding.

steps.title: Count the bonding pairs
steps.content:
Next, we look at the formula $\text{SF}_6$. There are 6 fluorine atoms bonded to the central sulfur atom. In this molecule, sulfur forms 6 single covalent bonds, one with each fluorine atom.

Each bond represents one **bonding pair** of electrons.

highlights.text: SF_6

steps.title: Calculate lone pairs
steps.content:
Now, we determine if there are any lone pairs on the sulfur atom. Sulfur started with 6 valence electrons and used all 6 to form the bonds with fluorine.

$$\text{Lone pairs} = \frac{\text{Valence } e^- - \text{Bonding } e^-}{2}$$

$$\text{Lone pairs} = \frac{6 - 6}{2} = 0$$

Since sulfur is using 12 electrons in total for these bonds, it is an example of an **expanded octet**, which is covered in **Topic S2.2.13**.

steps.title: Determine the molecular geometry
steps.content:
Finally, we add the bonding pairs and lone pairs to find the total number of **electron domains**:

$$\text{Total Domains} = 6 \text{ (bonding)} + 0 \text{ (lone)} = 6$$

According to **VSEPR theory** (Topic S2.2.4), 6 electron domains will arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. With 6 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs, the resulting molecular geometry is **octahedral**.

highlights.text: Octahedral geometry
6 bonding pairs

steps.title: Count sulfur's valence electrons
steps.content:
To start our Lewis diagram, we identify the central atom, which is sulfur ($S$). Sulfur is in Group 16 of the periodic table, so it has 6 valence electrons available for bonding.

This is a great example of an **expanded octet** (Topic S2.2.13), as sulfur is in Period 3 and can accommodate more than 8 electrons in its valence shell.

highlights.text: SF_6

steps.title: Determine bonding pairs
steps.content:
Next, we look at the atoms attached to the sulfur. There are 6 fluorine ($F$) atoms. To form the structure of $\text{SF}_6$, the sulfur atom forms a single covalent bond with each of the 6 fluorine atoms.

$$\text{Number of bonding pairs} = 6$$

steps.title: Check for lone pairs
steps.content:
Now, let's see if there are any non-bonding electrons (lone pairs) left on the central sulfur atom. 

Sulfur started with 6 valence electrons and used all 6 to form the bonds with fluorine. We can calculate the lone pairs on the central atom using this logic:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lone pairs} &= \frac{\text{Valence electrons} - \text{Bonding electrons}}{2} \\ &= \frac{6 - 6}{2} \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

So, there are **0 lone pairs** on the sulfur atom.

steps.title: Predict the molecular geometry
steps.content:
According to **VSEPR theory** (Topic S2.2.4), electron domains (bonding pairs and lone pairs) arrange themselves to stay as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion.

With 6 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs, the 6 domains point toward the corners of a regular octahedron. This results in a molecular geometry known as **octahedral**.

highlights.text: Predict the molecular geometry of SF_6 using VSEPR theory.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The molecular geometry of $\text{SF}_6$ is **octahedral**. 

In an exam, you get the mark by identifying the geometry and noting the 6 bonding pairs around the central atom, as shown in the markscheme.

highlights.text: Octahedral geometry (6 bonding pairs around central atom)

steps.title: Identify the central atom
steps.content: In the molecule $\text{SF}_6$, the central atom is sulfur ($\text{S}$). To apply the AXE method, we first need to determine the number of valence electrons for sulfur. Since sulfur is in Group 16 of the periodic table, it has 6 valence electrons.

steps.title: Determine bonding pairs (X)
steps.content:
Next, we identify how many atoms are bonded to the central sulfur atom. In $\text{SF}_6$, there are 6 fluorine ($\text{F}$) atoms. In AXE notation, **X** represents the number of bonding regions (atoms bonded to the central atom). Therefore:

$$X = 6$$

steps.title: Calculate lone pairs (E)
steps.content:
Now we determine if there are any lone pairs on the central sulfur atom, represented by **E**. 

Sulfur starts with 6 valence electrons and uses one electron for each of the 6 single bonds with fluorine. 

$$\text{Lone pair electrons} = 6 \text{ (valence)} - 6 \text{ (used in bonds)} = 0$$

Since there are no remaining valence electrons, there are zero lone pairs, so $E = 0$.

steps.title: Assign AXE classification
steps.content:
Combining our findings, the AXE classification for $\text{SF}_6$ is:

$$\text{AX}_6$$

This notation indicates a central atom (**A**) with 6 bonding pairs (**X**) and 0 lone pairs (**E**).

steps.title: Predict the molecular geometry
steps.content: According to VSEPR theory, a molecule with the classification $\text{AX}_6$ arranges its electron domains as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. This specific arrangement results in an **octahedral** geometry, where all bond angles are $90^\circ$.

highlights.text: Octahedral geometry (6 bonding pairs around central atom)

parts.content: State whether $\text{SF}_6$ is a polar or non-polar molecule.
parts.markscheme: Non-polar <marks>1</marks>

parts.content: Despite having polar $\text{S-F}$ bonds, $\text{SF}_6$ does not behave as a polar compound. Explain this apparent contradiction.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{S-F}$ bonds are polar due to the electronegativity difference between sulfur and fluorine <marks>1</marks>

The symmetrical octahedral shape causes the individual bond dipoles to cancel each other out, resulting in no net molecular dipole moment <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Explain S-F bond polarity
steps.content:
To understand why $\text{SF}_6$ is non-polar, we first need to look at the individual bonds. A bond is polar if there is a difference in electronegativity between the two atoms. Since fluorine is the most electronegative element, it pulls the shared electrons more strongly than sulfur.

This creates a bond dipole where sulfur has a partial positive charge ($\delta+$) and fluorine has a partial negative charge ($\delta-$).

$$ \text{S}^{\delta+} \rightarrow \text{F}^{\delta-} $$

highlights.text: S-F bonds are polar due to the electronegativity difference between sulfur and fluorine

steps.title: Determine molecular geometry
steps.content:
Next, we use **VSEPR theory** (Topic S2.2.4) to find the shape. Sulfur has 6 valence electrons and is bonded to 6 fluorine atoms with no lone pairs. 

This electron domain geometry is **octahedral**. In an octahedral molecule, all bond angles are $90^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$, creating a highly symmetrical structure.

steps.title: Analyze dipole cancellation
steps.content:
Even though each bond is polar, molecular polarity depends on the 3D arrangement of these bonds. 

In the symmetrical octahedral shape of $\text{SF}_6$, every $\text{S-F}$ bond dipole is exactly opposed by another dipole on the opposite side. Because these dipoles are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, they cancel each other out.

highlights.text: symmetrical octahedral shape causes the individual bond dipoles to cancel each other out

steps.title: Conclude on net dipole
steps.content:
Because the bond dipoles cancel, the **net molecular dipole moment is zero**. 

Therefore, despite having polar bonds, the overall molecule is non-polar. This is a common pattern for perfectly symmetrical molecules like $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{CO}_2$, and $\text{BF}_3$.

steps.title: Establish Bond Polarity
steps.content:
The first step is to recognize why the individual bonds are polar. Fluorine is the most electronegative element, while sulfur is less electronegative. This difference in electronegativity ($Δχ$) creates a bond dipole for each $\text{S-F}$ bond, where the electron density is pulled toward the fluorine atom.

This aligns with **Topic S2.2.5: Bond polarity**.

highlights.text: S-F bonds are polar due to the electronegativity difference between sulfur and fluorine

steps.title: Identify Molecular Geometry
steps.content: Using VSEPR theory (**Topic S2.2.4**), we find that $\text{SF}_6$ has six bonding pairs and zero lone pairs on the sulfur atom. This results in an **octahedral** electron domain and molecular geometry. In this arrangement, all bond angles are $90^\circ$ or $180^\circ$, creating a highly symmetrical structure.

steps.title: Apply Vector Summation
steps.content:
We can represent each polar bond as a **dipole vector** pointing from the sulfur to the fluorine. Because the octahedral shape is perfectly symmetrical, every dipole vector is opposed by an equal and opposite vector on the other side of the central atom ($180^\circ$ apart).

When we calculate the **vector sum** of these six dipoles:

$$\vec{\mu}_{net} = \sum \vec{\mu}_{S-F} = 0$$

The individual dipoles cancel each other out completely.

steps.title: Conclusion and Marks
steps.content:
Since the net molecular dipole moment is zero, $\text{SF}_6$ is a non-polar molecule. 

**Marking Breakdown:**
*   **1 Mark:** Stating bonds are polar due to electronegativity differences.
*   **1 Mark:** Explaining that the symmetrical octahedral shape leads to the cancellation of dipoles (net dipole = 0).

highlights.text: The symmetrical octahedral shape causes the individual bond dipoles to cancel each other out, resulting in no net molecular dipole moment

options.markscheme: Incorrect. In $\mathrm{BeCl_2}$ the central Be has 2 regions of electron density (two Be–Cl bonds) giving a linear arrangement and $sp$ hybridization, not $sp^3$.
Incorrect. In $\mathrm{BCl_3}$ the central B has 3 regions of electron density (three B–Cl bonds) giving a trigonal planar arrangement and $sp^2$ hybridization, not $sp^3$.
Correct. In $\mathrm{C_2H_2}$ (ethyne), each carbon has 2 regions of electron density (one C–H bond and one C≡C bond counted as one region) giving a linear arrangement and $sp$ hybridization.

Incorrect. In $\mathrm{NH_3}$ the central N has 4 regions of electron density (three N–H bonds and one lone pair) giving a tetrahedral electron-domain arrangement and $sp^3$ hybridization, not $sp^2$.

questions.specification:
Which of the following combinations best explains why the boiling point of hydrogen fluoride ($\text{HF}$) is much higher than that of fluorine ($\text{F}_2$)?

I. $\text{HF}$ exhibits hydrogen bonding while $\text{F}_2$ only has London dispersion forces.

II. The $\text{H--F}$ bond is more polar than the $\text{F--F}$ bond.

III. $\text{HF}$ and $\text{F}_2$ are both linear molecules.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Boiling point differences between HF and F2 are explained by intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding vs London dispersion) and how these affect physical properties like boiling point.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.5 Bond polarity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.5 Bond polarity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Statement II involves bond polarity (H–F more polar than F–F), which relates to molecular interactions and contributes to understanding why HF has stronger attractions than F2.

options.markscheme:
I is true: HF molecules form hydrogen bonds; $\text{F}_2$ is nonpolar and experiences only London dispersion forces, so HF has much stronger intermolecular attractions and a higher boiling point.

II is true: the H–F bond is highly polar whereas the F–F bond is nonpolar, supporting the presence of strong intermolecular attractions in HF.

III is true (both are diatomic and linear), but it does not help explain a boiling point difference. The key explanation involves hydrogen bonding (I), and II is also a correct contributing statement.
II is true, but without hydrogen bonding (I) this does not best explain the much higher boiling point of HF. III is true but not a factor in explaining the boiling point difference.

III is true but not relevant to explaining the boiling point difference. The best explanation is captured by I, with II as a supporting correct statement; including III does not improve the explanation.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.13 Expanded Octet of Electrons (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: SF4Cl2 has six bonding pairs around sulfur (an expanded octet/hypervalent central atom), which is part of expanded-octet bonding considerations when applying VSEPR to third-period elements.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Linear geometry would require 2 electron domains around S (e.g., $\text{AX}_2$), not 6 bonding pairs.
Incorrect. Tetrahedral molecular geometry corresponds to 4 electron domains (e.g., $\text{AX}_4$). Here S forms 6 S–X single bonds, giving 6 electron domains.
Incorrect. Trigonal bipyramidal geometry corresponds to 5 electron domains (e.g., $\text{AX}_5$). $\text{SF}_4\text{Cl}_2$ has 6 bonding pairs around S.

Correct. Total valence electrons: $6 + 4(7) + 2(7) = 48$. Six S–X single bonds use 12 e$^-$. The remaining 36 e$^-$ complete octets on the six terminal halogens (6×6 = 36), leaving 0 lone pairs on S. Thus there are 6 electron domains (all bonding) around S, giving an octahedral molecular geometry.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis (electron dot) structure of ethanedioic acid.
parts.markscheme:
![Lewis structure of ethanedioic acid](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/bdb47ca5-f6bf-463c-b843-4177c05f42e3.jpeg)

- Draw the structure with each carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) and to the other carbon atom. <marks>A1</marks>
- Show two lone pairs on each of the four oxygen atoms. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the functional groups
steps.content:
To draw the Lewis structure for ethanedioic acid, we first identify the functional groups from the molecular formula $(\text{COOH})_2$. The brackets and the subscript 2 tell us that there are **two carboxyl groups** ($-\text{COOH}$) linked together.

This structure is common in organic chemistry (Topic S3.2.2) and knowing the standard connectivity of a carboxyl group is the best starting point.

highlights.text: two carboxyl groups

steps.title: Draw the carbon-carbon backbone
steps.content:
Because the formula is $(\text{COOH})_2$, the two carbon atoms from the carboxyl groups must be bonded directly to each other to form the center of the molecule.

Start by drawing a single bond between the two carbon atoms:

$$\text{C}-\text{C}$$

steps.title: Attach carboxyl components
steps.content:
Next, we build the carboxyl groups on each carbon. Each carbon atom in a carboxyl group is double-bonded to one oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) group.

According to the markscheme, you must show this specific connectivity:
1. A double bond between carbon and one oxygen ($C=O$).
2. A single bond between carbon and the oxygen of the hydroxyl group ($C-O-H$).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{O} = &\text{C} - \text{C} = \text{O} \\ &| \quad \quad | \\ \text{HO} & \quad \quad \text{OH} \end{aligned}$$

(Note: The orientation doesn't matter as long as the bonds are correct).

highlights.text: Draw the structure with each carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) and to the other carbon atom.

steps.title: Add lone pairs
steps.content:
The final step for a complete Lewis structure is adding the non-bonding electrons (lone pairs). 

Oxygen has 6 valence electrons. In our structure:
- The double-bonded oxygens use 2 electrons for bonding, leaving 4 electrons (**2 lone pairs**).
- The hydroxyl oxygens use 2 electrons (one for the $C-O$ bond and one for the $O-H$ bond), leaving 4 electrons (**2 lone pairs**).

You must show these **two lone pairs on each of the four oxygen atoms** to earn the final mark.

highlights.text: Show two lone pairs on each of the four oxygen atoms.

steps.title: Final Lewis structure
steps.content:
Here is the completed Lewis structure for ethanedioic acid:

$$\begin{matrix} \phantom{.} & \mathbf{:}\ddot{\text{O}}\mathbf{:} & \phantom{.} & \mathbf{:}\ddot{\text{O}}\mathbf{:} & \phantom{.} \\ \phantom{.} & \parallel & \phantom{.} & \parallel & \phantom{.} \\ \text{H}-\ddot{\text{O}}- & \text{C} & - & \text{C} & -\ddot{\text{O}}-\text{H} \\ \phantom{.} & \mathbf{..} & \phantom{.} & \mathbf{..} & \phantom{.} \end{matrix}$$

Double-check that every atom (except Hydrogen) follows the octet rule (8 electrons) and that Carbon has exactly 4 bonds.

steps.title: Count total valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw a Lewis structure, we first calculate the total number of valence electrons available in the molecule. Using the group numbers from the periodic table:

- **Carbon (C):** Group 14 $\rightarrow 4$ electrons each
- **Oxygen (O):** Group 16 $\rightarrow 6$ electrons each
- **Hydrogen (H):** Group 1 $\rightarrow 1$ electron each

For ethanedioic acid, $(\text{COOH})_2$ (or $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4\text{H}_2$):
$$\text{Total} = 2(4) + 4(6) + 2(1) = 34 \text{ electrons}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2*4 + 4*6 + 2*1 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total sum of valence electrons.

highlights.text: (COOH)2

steps.title: Arrange the skeletal structure
steps.content:
Next, we arrange the atoms. Ethanedioic acid consists of two carboxyl groups ($\text{-COOH}$) linked together. The two carbon atoms bond to each other in the center. Each carbon is then bonded to one oxygen atom and one hydroxyl ($\text{-OH}$) group.

Skeletal layout:  
$\text{H-O-C-C-O-H}$ with an additional $\text{O}$ attached to each $\text{C}$.

steps.title: Form bonds and distribute electrons
steps.content:
We start by placing single bonds between all connected atoms. Each single bond represents 2 electrons. 

1. Connect $\text{C-C}$, two $\text{C-O}$, two $\text{C-OH}$, and two $\text{O-H}$. This uses 7 single bonds (14 electrons).
2. Subtract these from our total: $34 - 14 = 20$ electrons remaining.
3. Distribute these 20 electrons as lone pairs to satisfy the octet rule for the oxygen atoms. 

However, in this single-bonded arrangement, the carbon atoms only have 6 electrons each. To satisfy the **octet rule** for carbon, we must move one lone pair from each of the terminal oxygen atoms to form a $\text{C=O}$ double bond.

ti84.instructions: Enter 34 - 14 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate remaining electrons after forming initial single bonds.

steps.title: Finalize the Lewis structure
steps.content:
Now we ensure every atom has a full outer shell (octet for C and O, duet for H) and all 34 electrons are shown.

- Each Carbon has 4 bonds (8 electrons).
- Each Carbonyl Oxygen ($\text{C=O}$) has 2 bonds and 2 lone pairs (8 electrons).
- Each Hydroxyl Oxygen ($\text{C-O-H}$) has 2 bonds and 2 lone pairs (8 electrons).

Your final drawing should look like this:

$$\begin{matrix} \phantom{..} & \text{:O:} & \phantom{..} & \text{:O:} & \phantom{..} \\ \phantom{..} & \parallel & \phantom{..} & \parallel & \phantom{..} \\ \text{H-O:} & \text{C} & \text{---} & \text{C} & \text{:O-H} \\ \phantom{..} & \phantom{..} & \phantom{..} & \phantom{..} & \phantom{..} \end{matrix}$$
*(Note: Ensure you draw two lone pairs on every oxygen atom!)*

highlights.text: Draw the structure with each carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group (-OH) and to the other carbon atom.
Show two lone pairs on each of the four oxygen atoms.

parts.content: Identify the type of bonding between the carbon and oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the bonding as covalent. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Accept "polar covalent". The $C=O$ double bond consists of one sigma $(\sigma)$ bond and one pi $(\pi)$ bond.</note>

steps.title: Identify the elements involved
steps.content: To determine the type of bonding, we first look at the specific atoms involved in the bond within the carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$): **Carbon ($C$)** and **Oxygen ($O$)**.

highlights.text: carbon and oxygen atoms
carboxyl group

steps.title: Classify the element types
steps.content:
Using the Periodic Table (as discussed in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**), we classify each element:

*   **Carbon ($C$)**: Located in Group 14, it is a **non-metal**.
*   **Oxygen ($O$)**: Located in Group 16, it is also a **non-metal**.

steps.title: Determine the bonding type
steps.content:
According to the models of bonding (**Topic S2.2: The covalent model**), when two **non-metals** react, they achieve stability by **sharing electron pairs** rather than transferring them.

Since both carbon and oxygen are non-metals, the bond formed between them is **covalent**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The bonding between the carbon and oxygen atoms is **covalent**.

*(Note: While the markscheme accepts 'polar covalent' due to the difference in electronegativity, the fundamental classification based on element types is covalent.)*

highlights.text: Identify the bonding as covalent.

steps.title: Identify atoms and values
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type between carbon ($C$) and oxygen ($O$), we first find their electronegativity values ($\chi$) from the IB Data Booklet (typically Table 8). 

Electronegativity is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract a bonding pair of electrons.

highlights.text: carbon and oxygen atoms

steps.title: Calculate electronegativity difference
steps.content:
Using the Pauling scale values from the data booklet:
- Carbon ($\chi_C$) = $2.6$
- Oxygen ($\chi_O$) = $3.4$

We calculate the difference ($\Delta\chi$):

$$\Delta\chi = \chi_O - \chi_C = 3.4 - 2.6 = 0.8$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction directly on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in electronegativity values.

steps.title: Analyze the bonding type
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we use the **bonding continuum** to classify bonds based on $\Delta\chi$:
- $\Delta\chi < 1.8$: Covalent bonding
- $0.5 \le \Delta\chi < 1.8$: Polar covalent bonding
- $\Delta\chi \ge 1.8$: Ionic bonding

Since our calculated value is $0.8$, the bond is classified as **polar covalent**. This means the electrons are shared between the atoms, but they are pulled more strongly toward the oxygen atom.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: Based on the electronegativity difference and the fact that both carbon and oxygen are non-metals, the bonding is **covalent** (specifically polar covalent).

highlights.text: Identify the bonding as covalent.

steps.title: Analyze the molecule's nature
steps.content: The question asks about ethanedioic acid, which is an organic compound. In organic chemistry, we study molecules where atoms are held together by intramolecular bonds. For most organic molecules and their functional groups, such as the carboxyl group ($-\text{COOH}$), these bonds are formed by the sharing of electrons.

highlights.text: ethanedioic acid (($\text{COOH}$)$_2$)
carboxyl groups

steps.title: Classify the atoms involved
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type, look at the elements involved: carbon (C) and oxygen (O). Both of these are non-metals. 

This is a key concept from **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, which explains that bonds between non-metal atoms typically involve the sharing of electron pairs.

steps.title: Deduce the bonding type
steps.content:
Because carbon and oxygen are both non-metals sharing electrons to achieve a stable electron configuration, the bond between them is **covalent**.

In the carboxyl group, this specifically includes:
- A $C=O$ double bond (one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond).
- A $C-O$ single bond (one $\sigma$ bond).

Since oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, you can also describe these specifically as **polar covalent** bonds.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The bonding between the carbon and oxygen atoms is **covalent**.

highlights.text: Identify the bonding as covalent.

parts.content: State the number of sigma ($\sigma$) and pi ($\pi$) bonds present in one molecule of ethanedioic acid.
parts.markscheme:
- State the number of sigma bonds as 7 (four $\text{C-O}$, one $\text{C-C}$, and two $\text{O-H}$ bonds). <marks>A1</marks>
- State the number of pi bonds as 2 (one in each $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Visualize the molecule
steps.content:
To find the number of sigma ($\sigma$) and pi ($\pi$) bonds, we first need to understand the structural layout of **ethanedioic acid**, $(\text{COOH})_2$. This molecule consists of two carboxyl groups ($-\text{COOH}$) linked together by a carbon-carbon single bond.

This topic is covered in **S2.2.15: Sigma and Pi Bonds**. Remember:
- Every single bond is **1 sigma bond**.
- Every double bond consists of **1 sigma bond** and **1 pi bond**.

highlights.text: ethanedioic acid
$$(\text{COOH})_2$$

steps.title: Draw the displayed formula
steps.content:
Using the **Full Displayed Formula Analysis** approach, we draw every atom and every bond individually. This ensures we don't miss any hidden bonds, like the ones between Oxygen and Hydrogen.

The structure looks like this:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{H} - \text{O} - &\text{C} = \text{O} \\ &| \\ &\text{C} = \text{O} \\ \text{H} - \text{O} - & \end{aligned}$$

In this drawing, we can clearly see the $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond, the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bonds, and the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ single bonds.

steps.title: Count the sigma bonds
steps.content:
Now, let's count the **sigma ($\sigma$) bonds**. We count every single bond and exactly one bond from every double bond.

1.  **One** $\text{C}-\text{C}$ single bond.
2.  **Two** $\text{C}-\text{O}$ single bonds.
3.  **Two** $\text{O}-\text{H}$ single bonds.
4.  **Two** $\sigma$ bonds (one from each of the two $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bonds).

Total sigma bonds = $1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7$.

ti84.instructions: Add the bond counts: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of sigma bonds.

highlights.text: State the number of sigma bonds as 7

steps.title: Count the pi bonds
steps.content:
Next, we count the **pi ($\pi$) bonds**. Pi bonds are the "extra" bonds found in double or triple bonds.

In ethanedioic acid, we have two carboxyl groups, each containing one $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bond. 
- Each $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bond contains **one** pi bond.

Total pi bonds = $1 + 1 = 2$.

highlights.text: State the number of pi bonds as 2

steps.title: Final answer state
steps.content:
Based on our full structural analysis, one molecule of ethanedioic acid contains:

- **7 sigma ($\sigma$) bonds**
- **2 pi ($\pi$) bonds**

This matches the markscheme requirements for both marks.

steps.title: Visualize the functional groups
steps.content:
To count the bonds using the **Bond Inventory approach**, we first need to visualize how ethanedioic acid $(\text{COOH})_2$ is put together. This molecule consists of two carboxyl groups ($-\text{COOH}$) linked by a single carbon-carbon bond ($-\text{C}-\text{C}-$).

The full structural formula looks like this:

$$\text{HO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OH}$$

highlights.text: (\text{COOH})_2

steps.title: Inventory the bond types
steps.content:
Based on the functional groups, let's list every individual bond present in the molecule:

*   **1** $\text{C}-\text{C}$ single bond (linking the two carboxyl groups).
*   **2** $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bonds (one in each carboxyl group).
*   **2** $\text{C}-\text{O}$ single bonds (one in each carboxyl group).
*   **2** $\text{O}-\text{H}$ single bonds (one in each carboxyl group).

steps.title: Calculate Sigma bonds
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry (Topic 2.2.15), we learn that every single bond is a sigma ($\sigma$) bond, and every double bond consists of one $\sigma$ and one pi ($\pi$) bond. 

Let's count the $\sigma$ contribution from each bond type identified:
*   $\text{C}-\text{C}$: $1 \text{ bond} \times 1\sigma = 1\sigma$
*   $\text{C}=\text{O}$: $2 \text{ bonds} \times 1\sigma = 2\sigma$
*   $\text{C}-\text{O}$: $2 \text{ bonds} \times 1\sigma = 2\sigma$
*   $\text{O}-\text{H}$: $2 \text{ bonds} \times 1\sigma = 2\sigma$

Total Sigma ($\sigma$) bonds: $1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7$

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the sigma bond contributions: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2

ti84.instructions: Add the number of sigma bonds from each group: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2

calculator.description: Summing the sigma bonds

steps.title: Calculate Pi bonds
steps.content:
Pi ($\pi$) bonds only occur in multiple bonds (double or triple). 

*   Single bonds ($\text{C}-\text{C}, \text{C}-\text{O}, \text{O}-\text{H}$) have **0** $\pi$ bonds.
*   Double bonds ($\text{C}=\text{O}$) have **1** $\pi$ bond each.

Since there are two $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bonds:
Total Pi ($\pi$) bonds: $2 \times 1 = 2$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Following the markscheme, you earn one mark for each total:

*   **Sigma ($\sigma$) bonds: 7**
*   **Pi ($\pi$) bonds: 2**

highlights.text: State the number of sigma bonds as 7 (four \text{C-O}, one \text{C-C}, and two \text{O-H} bonds).
State the number of pi bonds as 2 (one in each \text{C}=\text{O} bond).

parts.content: Deduce the molecular geometry around one of the carbon atoms in ethanedioic acid.
parts.markscheme:
- Deduce the molecular geometry around the carbon atom as trigonal planar. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Draw the Lewis structure
steps.content:
To determine the molecular geometry, we first look at the Lewis structure of one carboxyl group in ethanedioic acid ($(\text{COOH})_2$). This is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

In each group, the carbon atom is bonded to:
- One carbon atom (via a single bond)
- One oxygen atom (via a double bond, $\text{C=O}$)
- One oxygen atom (via a single bond, $\text{C-OH}$)

$$\text{HO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{OH}$$

highlights.text: ethanedioic acid ($(\text{COOH})_2$)

steps.title: Count electron domains
steps.content:
According to **VSEPR Theory**, we count the number of electron domains (regions of negative charge) around the central carbon atom. Remember that a single bond, a double bond, and a triple bond each count as **one** electron domain.

Around one carbon atom, we have:
- 1 domain from the $\text{C-C}$ single bond
- 1 domain from the $\text{C=O}$ double bond
- 1 domain from the $\text{C-O}$ single bond
- 0 lone pairs on the carbon atom

Total electron domains = $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$

highlights.text: VSEPR theory

steps.title: Determine the molecular geometry
steps.content:
When a central atom has **3 electron domains** and **0 lone pairs**, the domains repel each other to be as far apart as possible, pointing toward the corners of an equilateral triangle.

This arrangement results in a **trigonal planar** geometry with bond angles of approximately $120^\circ$.

highlights.text: Deduce the molecular geometry around the carbon atom as trigonal planar.

steps.title: Analyze the carbon's bonding
steps.content:
To find the molecular geometry, let's look at the structure of one of the carboxyl groups ($\text{-COOH}$) in ethanedioic acid. The central carbon atom is bonded to:

1. The other carbon atom (a single bond).
2. One oxygen atom via a double bond ($=O$).
3. One oxygen atom via a single bond (the $\text{-OH}$ group).

This means the carbon atom is directly bonded to **three** other atoms and has **zero** lone pairs.

highlights.text: molecular geometry around one of the carbon atoms in ethanedioic acid

steps.title: Determine the hybridization
steps.content:
Since the carbon atom has three bonding partners and no lone pairs, it requires three hybrid orbitals to accommodate these electron domains. 

This corresponds to **$sp^2$ hybridization**. In $sp^2$ hybridization, one $s$ orbital and two $p$ orbitals mix to form three equivalent hybrid orbitals that spread out as far as possible to minimize repulsion.

steps.title: Identify the geometry
steps.content:
According to VSEPR theory and hybridization models, an atom with **$sp^2$ hybridization** and no lone pairs arranges its bonds in a **trigonal planar** shape to achieve maximum separation (with bond angles of approximately $120^\circ$).

Therefore, the molecular geometry around the carbon atom is **trigonal planar**.

parts.content: Explain why the bond angle around the carbon atom in the carboxyl group is approximately $120^{\circ}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that there are three regions of electron density (electron domains) around each carbon atom. <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that these regions arrange themselves in a trigonal planar shape to minimize repulsion, resulting in bond angles of approximately $120^{\circ}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify electron domains
steps.content:
To determine the bond angle using VSEPR theory, we first look at the central carbon atom in the carboxyl group ($\text{-COOH}$). This carbon is bonded to:
1. An oxygen atom via a double bond ($C=O$).
2. An oxygen atom via a single bond ($C-OH$).
3. The other carbon atom in the molecule via a single bond ($C-C$).

In VSEPR theory, double bonds are treated the same as single bonds—as a single "region" of electron density. Therefore, there are **three regions of electron density (electron domains)** around the carbon atom.

highlights.text: three regions of electron density (electron domains) around each carbon atom

steps.title: Apply VSEPR repulsion principle
steps.content: Electron domains consist of negatively charged electrons that naturally repel one another. To **minimize repulsion**, these domains arrange themselves to be as far apart from each other as possible in three-dimensional space.

highlights.text: minimize repulsion

steps.title: Determine geometry and angle
steps.content: When there are three electron domains and no lone pairs on the central atom, the furthest apart they can get is in a **trigonal planar shape**. In this geometric arrangement, the space is divided equally ($360^{\circ} / 3$), resulting in **bond angles of approximately $120^{\circ}$**.

ti84.instructions: Divide 360 by 3 to find the angle for a circle split into three equal parts.

calculator.description: Calculate the ideal bond angle for 3 equally spaced domains.

highlights.text: trigonal planar shape
bond angles of approximately 120^{\circ}

steps.title: Count electron domains
steps.content:
First, we look at the central carbon atom in the carboxyl group ($-COOH$). It is bonded to three other atoms: one oxygen via a double bond ($C=O$), one oxygen via a single bond ($C-OH$), and the adjacent carbon atom. 

In chemical bonding theory, single, double, and triple bonds all count as **one region of electron density** (electron domain). Since this carbon has no lone pairs, it has a total of **three electron domains**.

steps.title: Identify sp2 hybridization
steps.content:
When an atom has three electron domains, it undergoes **$sp^2$ hybridization**. This process involves mixing one $2s$ orbital and two $2p$ orbitals to form three equivalent $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals. 

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.16: Hybridization and Bonding**.

steps.title: Determine molecular geometry
steps.content: To minimize repulsion between the electrons in these domains, the three $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals arrange themselves as far apart as possible in a 2D plane. This results in a **trigonal planar** geometry.

steps.title: Confirm the bond angle
steps.content:
In a perfect trigonal planar arrangement, the bond angles are exactly $120^{\circ}$ because the $360^{\circ}$ space around the atom is divided equally among the three domains.

$$\text{Bond Angle} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{3} = 120^{\circ}$$

This explains why the angle is approximately $120^{\circ}$. The slight variation from exactly $120^{\circ}$ in real molecules occurs because a double bond ($C=O$) exerts slightly more repulsion than a single bond.

gdc.instructions: Divide the total degrees in a circle (360) by the number of electron domains (3).

ti84.instructions: Divide 360 by 3.

calculator.description: Calculate the ideal bond angle for three equally spaced domains.

highlights.text: approximately 120^{\circ}

options.markscheme: The bond angle in ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) is approximately $107^\circ$ due to the presence of one lone pair.

The bond angle in water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) is approximately $104.5^\circ$. It has the smallest bond angle among the choices because it has two lone pairs on the oxygen atom, which exert greater repulsion on the bonding pairs than the single lone pair in $\text{NH}_3$ or the zero lone pairs in $\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{BF}_3$.
The bond angle in boron trifluoride ($\text{BF}_3$) is $120^\circ$ as it has a trigonal planar geometry.
The bond angle in methane ($\text{CH}_4$) is $109.5^\circ$ as it has a perfect tetrahedral geometry.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis structure of $\mathrm{CH_2O}$.
parts.markscheme:
Central Carbon atom double bonded to Oxygen and single bonded to two Hydrogen atoms; Oxygen atom has two lone pairs.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate total valence electrons
steps.content:
To start the **Total Valence Electrons Method**, we first sum the valence electrons for every atom in $\mathrm{CH_2O}$ based on their group in the Periodic Table:

*   **Carbon (C):** Group 14 $\rightarrow$ 4 valence electrons
*   **Hydrogen (H):** Group 1 $\rightarrow$ 1 valence electron $\times 2 = 2$ valence electrons
*   **Oxygen (O):** Group 16 $\rightarrow$ 6 valence electrons

$$\text{Total} = 4 + 2 + 6 = 12 \text{ electrons}$$

steps.title: Identify the central atom
steps.content:
Next, we identify the central atom. This is usually the least electronegative element (excluding Hydrogen, which can only form one bond). 

In this molecule, **Carbon** is the central atom. We place the Oxygen and the two Hydrogen atoms around it and connect them with single bonds:

*   3 single bonds (C-H, C-H, C-O) use $3 \times 2 = 6$ electrons.
*   Remaining electrons: $12 - 6 = 6$ electrons.

steps.title: Complete octets and duets
steps.content:
Now, we distribute the remaining 6 electrons to satisfy the octet rule (8 electrons) for Carbon and Oxygen, and the duet rule (2 electrons) for Hydrogen.

1.  **Hydrogen:** Each H already has 2 electrons from its single bond. They are stable.
2.  **Oxygen:** We place the remaining 6 electrons as 3 lone pairs on Oxygen. Oxygen now has an octet (2 from the bond + 6 lone electrons).
3.  **Check Carbon:** Carbon only has 6 electrons (from 3 single bonds). To reach an octet, we must move one lone pair from Oxygen to form a **double bond** with Carbon.

steps.title: Draw the final structure
steps.content:
The final Lewis structure shows Carbon at the center with:
*   A **double bond** to Oxygen ($C=O$).
*   Two **single bonds** to Hydrogen ($C-H$).
*   **Two lone pairs** remaining on the Oxygen atom.

This arrangement ensures every atom has a full outer shell and we have used exactly 12 valence electrons. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Central Carbon atom double bonded to Oxygen and single bonded to two Hydrogen atoms; Oxygen atom has two lone pairs.

steps.title: Identify bonding capacities
steps.content:
To build the molecule using the **Standard Bonding Capacity Method**, we first identify how many bonds each atom typically forms to satisfy its octet (or duet for hydrogen):

*   **Carbon (C):** Needs 4 bonds (Group 14).
*   **Oxygen (O):** Needs 2 bonds and has 2 lone pairs (Group 16).
*   **Hydrogen (H):** Needs 1 bond.

This is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, where we look at how atoms share electrons to reach stable configurations.

steps.title: Determine the central atom
steps.content:
The atom with the highest bonding capacity usually acts as the central atom because it can connect to the most other atoms. 

In $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$, **Carbon** (which needs 4 bonds) will be our central atom.

steps.title: Assemble the skeletal structure
steps.content:
Now, we connect the external atoms to the central Carbon atom based on their bonding needs:

1.  Connect the two **Hydrogen** atoms to Carbon using single bonds (each H uses 1 bond). 
2.  Connect the **Oxygen** atom to Carbon. Since Carbon has 2 remaining bonding slots and Oxygen needs 2 bonds, they form a **double bond** ($C=O$).

At this stage, the Carbon atom has a total of 4 bonds (2 single and 1 double), satisfying its bonding capacity.

steps.title: Add lone pairs
steps.content:
Finally, we must ensure every atom has a full outer shell. 

While Carbon and Hydrogen are satisfied, Oxygen requires **two lone pairs** of electrons to complete its octet (8 electrons total). Adding these results in the final Lewis structure:

$$\begin{matrix} \quad H \\ \quad | \\ H - C = \ddot{O}: \end{matrix}$$

(Note: The specific orientation of the H atoms doesn't matter for the Lewis structure, as long as the connectivity and lone pairs are correct).

highlights.text: Central Carbon atom double bonded to Oxygen and single bonded to two Hydrogen atoms; Oxygen atom has two lone pairs.

parts.content: State the number of electron domains around the carbon atom.
parts.markscheme:
3 electron domains
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Determine total valence electrons
steps.content:
To analyze the bonding in formaldehyde ($CH_2O$), we first count the total number of valence electrons available:

- **Carbon (Group 14):** 4 electrons
- **Hydrogen (Group 1):** $1 \times 2 = 2$ electrons
- **Oxygen (Group 16):** 6 electrons

Total valence electrons = $4 + 2 + 6 = 12$ electrons.

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the valence electrons.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the valence electrons on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of valence electrons for the molecule.

steps.title: Construct the Lewis structure
steps.content:
Based on the number of valence electrons and the bonding requirements, we place Carbon as the central atom. 

Following the **Lewis Structure Analysis** approach, the carbon atom forms:
- Two **single bonds** with the two Hydrogen atoms.
- One **double bond** with the Oxygen atom.

This uses all 12 valence electrons (including the lone pairs on oxygen) and ensures Carbon reaches a stable octet (8 electrons in its valence shell).

highlights.text: carbon atom is connected via two single bonds to hydrogen and one double bond to oxygen

steps.title: Identify electron domains
steps.content:
In **Topic S2.2.4: VSEPR theory**, an 'electron domain' is any region where electrons are concentrated around a central atom. This includes:
- Any single bond
- Any double bond
- Any triple bond
- Any lone pair

Looking at the central Carbon atom in formaldehyde:
- It has **2 single bonds** (to Hydrogen).
- It has **1 double bond** (to Oxygen).
- It has **0 lone pairs**.

Adding these together:
$$1 + 2 = 3 \text{ electron domains}$$

highlights.text: State the number of electron domains around the carbon atom.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Therefore, the number of electron domains around the carbon atom is **3**. 

In an IB exam, simply stating the number is sufficient for the mark as indicated by the markscheme.

highlights.text: 3 electron domains

steps.title: Identify the central atom
steps.content: In the formaldehyde molecule ($\mathrm{CH_2O}$), Carbon ($\text{C}$) is the central atom. To determine the electron geometry, we first need to look at how many things are surrounding this central atom.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{CH_2O}$

steps.title: Count the bonded atoms
steps.content:
First, we identify the number of atoms directly bonded to the carbon atom. In $\mathrm{CH_2O}$, Carbon is bonded to:
- Two Hydrogen ($\text{H}$) atoms
- One Oxygen ($\text{O}$) atom

This gives us a total of **3 bonded atoms**. Remember, in VSEPR theory, a double bond (like the one between C and O) still only counts as **one** electron domain.

steps.title: Determine lone pairs
steps.content:
Next, we check for lone pairs on the central Carbon atom. 

Carbon is in Group 14, so it has 4 valence electrons. In this molecule, it uses all 4 electrons to form bonds (two single bonds to H and one double bond to O). 

Since all valence electrons are involved in bonding, there are **0 lone pairs** on the carbon atom.

steps.title: Calculate total electron domains
steps.content:
Finally, we apply the VSEPR bonding rule by adding the number of bonded atoms and the number of lone pairs together:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Electron Domains} &= \text{Atoms Bonded} + \text{Lone Pairs} \\ &= 3 + 0 \\ &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

There are **3 electron domains** around the carbon atom, which leads to a trigonal planar electron domain geometry.

parts.content: Deduce the approximate bond angles around the carbon atom.
parts.markscheme:
Approximately $120^\circ$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Count total valence electrons
steps.content:
To determine the shape of the molecule, we first need to know how many valence electrons are available for bonding in **Formaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH_2O}$)**. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Carbon (Group 14)} &= 4 \text{ electrons} \\ \text{Hydrogen (Group 1)} &= 1 \times 2 = 2 \text{ electrons} \\ \text{Oxygen (Group 16)} &= 6 \text{ electrons} \\ \hline \text{Total} &= 12 \text{ valence electrons} \end{aligned}$$

This knowledge comes from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

highlights.text: Formaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH_2O}$)

steps.title: Draw the Lewis structure
steps.content:
Using the 12 valence electrons, we place Carbon as the central atom. It forms single bonds with the two Hydrogen atoms and a double bond with the Oxygen atom to ensure every atom (except Hydrogen) follows the octet rule.

In the structure: 
- The $\text{C=O}$ double bond uses 4 electrons. 
- The two $\text{C-H}$ single bonds use 4 electrons. 
- Oxygen has two lone pairs to complete its octet (4 electrons).

Total used: $4 + 4 + 4 = 12$ electrons.

steps.title: Count central electron domains
steps.content:
Now we apply **VSEPR theory** (Topic S2.2.4). We need to count the number of electron domains around the central Carbon atom. 

An electron domain can be a single bond, a double bond, a triple bond, or a lone pair. Around the Carbon in $\mathrm{CH_2O}$, we have:
- 2 single bonds (to H atoms)
- 1 double bond (to the O atom)

This gives a total of **3 electron domains**.

steps.title: Determine bond angles
steps.content:
According to VSEPR theory, 3 electron domains will arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. This results in a **trigonal planar** electron geometry.

For a trigonal planar arrangement with no lone pairs on the central atom, the ideal bond angle is exactly $120^\circ$.

Therefore, the **approximate bond angles around the carbon atom** are $120^\circ$.

highlights.text: approximate bond angles around the carbon atom

steps.title: Analyze the bonding structure
steps.content:
To find the bond angles, we first look at how the central carbon atom in formaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH_2O}$) is bonded. Carbon has 4 valence electrons and forms:
- One **double bond** with the oxygen atom ($C=O$)
- Two **single bonds** with the hydrogen atoms ($C-H$)

This gives the carbon atom a total of **three electron domains**.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{CH_2O}$

steps.title: Determine the hybridization
steps.content:
Based on the number of electron domains, we can determine the hybridization of the carbon atom. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.16: Hybridization and Bonding**.

Since there are 3 electron domains, the carbon atom uses one $s$ orbital and two $p$ orbitals to form three equivalent hybrid orbitals. This is known as **$sp^2$ hybridization**.

steps.title: Relate hybridization to geometry
steps.content:
In $sp^2$ hybridization, the three hybrid orbitals arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize electron repulsion (following VSEPR theory). 

This arrangement results in a **trigonal planar** geometry around the carbon atom.

steps.title: Deduce the bond angle
steps.content:
In a perfect trigonal planar geometry, the $360^\circ$ around the central atom is divided equally among the three domains:

$$\text{Bond Angle} = \frac{360^\circ}{3} = 120^\circ$$

Therefore, the approximate bond angles ($H-C-H$ and $H-C-O$) are $120^\circ$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 360 divided by 3.

calculator.description: Calculate the ideal bond angle for 3 equal domains.

parts.content: State the reason why $\mathrm{CH_2O}$ is polar.
parts.markscheme:
The C=O bond is polar and the molecule is not symmetrical, resulting in a net dipole moment.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify bond polarity
steps.content: To understand why a molecule is polar, we first look at the individual bonds. In formaldehyde ($CH_2O$), the carbon atom is double-bonded to an oxygen atom. Because oxygen is significantly more electronegative than carbon, the $C=O$ bond is **polar**, with the electron density pulled towards the oxygen atom.

highlights.text: The C=O bond is polar

steps.title: Determine molecular geometry
steps.content:
Next, we consider the shape of the molecule using VSEPR theory. The central carbon atom has three electron domains (one double bond and two single bonds) and no lone pairs. This results in a **trigonal planar** molecular geometry with bond angles of approximately $120^\circ$. 

You can review these shapes in **Topic S2.2.4: VSEPR theory**.

steps.title: Analyze molecular symmetry
steps.content:
For a molecule to be non-polar, its bond dipoles must cancel out. This only happens if the molecule is highly symmetrical and all surrounding atoms are identical. 

In $CH_2O$, the molecule is **asymmetrical** because the $C=O$ bond dipole is much stronger than the $C-H$ bond dipoles. Because the distribution of charge is not uniform, the dipoles do not cancel out.

highlights.text: molecule is not symmetrical

steps.title: Conclusion: Net dipole moment
steps.content:
Since there is an overall imbalance in the distribution of electron density, the molecule has a **net dipole moment**. 

According to the markscheme, the single mark is awarded for mentioning both the polar bond and the lack of symmetry.

highlights.text: resulting in a net dipole moment

steps.title: Compare electronegativity values
steps.content:
To understand why formaldehyde is polar, we first look at the electronegativity of the atoms involved. Electronegativity is a measure of how strongly an atom attracts a bonding pair of electrons. 

In the $\text{C=O}$ bond, Oxygen is significantly more electronegative than Carbon. This creates a **bond dipole**, where the electrons in the double bond are pulled more toward the Oxygen atom.

highlights.text: The C=O bond is polar

steps.title: Analyze charge distribution
steps.content:
Because the electron density is shifted toward the more electronegative Oxygen atom, the charge is distributed unequally. 

- The Oxygen atom gains a partial negative charge ($$\delta-$$).
- The Carbon atom is left with a partial positive charge ($$\delta+$$).

This unequal distribution of electron density creates a permanent dipole along the $\text{C=O}$ bond.

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content:
Next, we consider the molecular geometry. Formaldehyde has a trigonal planar shape. For a molecule to be non-polar, it must be perfectly symmetrical so that the individual bond dipoles cancel each other out.

However, in $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$, the Carbon is bonded to two Hydrogen atoms and one Oxygen atom. Because the terminal atoms are not the same, the molecule is **not symmetrical** in a way that allows the dipoles to cancel.

highlights.text: molecule is not symmetrical

steps.title: Identify the net dipole
steps.content:
Since the $\text{C=O}$ bond is polar and the molecular geometry does not allow the dipoles to cancel, the molecule has an overall **net dipole moment**. This shift in the center of charge makes the entire molecule polar.

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.6: Molecular polarity** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: resulting in a net dipole moment

options.markscheme: Incorrect: the molecule has 4 $\ce{C#N}$ bonds (8 $\pi$, 4 $\sigma$), one $\ce{C=C}$ bond (1 $\pi$, 1 $\sigma$), and four $\ce{C-C}$ single bonds (4 $\sigma$), giving totals of 9 $\pi$ and 9 $\sigma$.

Correct: 4 $\ce{C#N}$ bonds contribute 8 $\pi$ and 4 $\sigma$; the central $\ce{C=C}$ contributes 1 $\pi$ and 1 $\sigma$; four $\ce{C-C}$ single bonds contribute 4 $\sigma$. Totals: 9 $\pi$ and 9 $\sigma$.
Incorrect: this undercounts the $\pi$ bonds from the four $\ce{C#N}$ triple bonds (each has 2 $\pi$ bonds).
Incorrect: while the total $\sigma$ count is 9, the total $\pi$ count is 9 (not 5) because each $\ce{C#N}$ triple bond contributes 2 $\pi$ bonds.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis structure of $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, indicating all bonds and lone pairs.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw the Lewis structure showing a triple bond between carbon atoms and single bonds to hydrogen: $\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Calculate total valence electrons
steps.content:
First, we determine the total number of valence electrons available for bonding. Carbon is in Group 14 and has 4 valence electrons. Hydrogen is in Group 1 and has 1 valence electron. 

For $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$:
$$\text{Total electrons} = 2(4) + 2(1) = 10 \text{ electrons}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 4 + 2 * 1 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of valence electrons for C2H2.

steps.title: Draw the skeletal structure
steps.content:
Next, we arrange the atoms to form the skeletal structure. Carbon atoms are usually central in hydrocarbons. We connect them with single bonds to start:

$$\text{H}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{H}$$

This structure uses 3 single bonds. Since each bond represents 2 electrons, we have used:
$$3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ electrons}$$

highlights.text: draw the initial H-C-C-H skeletal structure with single bonds

steps.title: Distribute remaining electrons
steps.content:
We subtract the bonding electrons from the total to find how many are left to distribute:
$$10 - 6 = 4 \text{ electrons remaining}$$

Hydrogen only needs a duet (2 electrons), which it already has from the single bonds. We place the remaining 4 electrons as lone pairs on the carbon atoms to try and satisfy their octets.

steps.title: Satisfy the octet rule
steps.content:
Currently, each carbon atom only has 6 electrons (4 from two bonds and 2 from a lone pair). To satisfy the octet rule (8 electrons per carbon), we must share the lone pairs to form multiple bonds between the carbon atoms.

Moving two pairs of electrons from the carbons into the central bond creates a **triple bond**.

highlights.text: satisfy the octet rule for carbon and the duet rule for hydrogen

steps.title: Final Lewis structure
steps.content:
The final structure shows a triple bond between the carbons and single bonds to the hydrogens. Each carbon now has 8 electrons, and each hydrogen has 2.

$$\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$$

This matches the requirement for 1 mark on the IB markscheme.

highlights.text: Draw the Lewis structure showing a triple bond between carbon atoms and single bonds to hydrogen: $\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$

steps.title: Determine bonding capacities
steps.content:
To draw the Lewis structure using the **Atomic Valency Approach**, we first identify the standard number of bonds each atom prefers to form (its valency):

*   **Carbon (C):** Located in Group 14, it has 4 valence electrons and requires **4 bonds** to complete its octet.
*   **Hydrogen (H):** Located in Group 1, it has 1 valence electron and requires **1 bond** to complete its outer shell (duet).

steps.title: Arrange the atoms
steps.content:
In a hydrocarbon like $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ (acetylene), the carbon atoms typically form the central skeleton. We arrange the two carbon atoms in the center and distribute the two hydrogen atoms symmetrically on either side:

$$\text{H} \quad \text{C} \quad \text{C} \quad \text{H}$$

highlights.text: acetylene, C2H2

steps.title: Satisfy hydrogen's valency
steps.content:
Since each hydrogen atom can only form one bond, we connect each hydrogen to its adjacent carbon atom with a single bond:

$$\text{H}-\text{C} \quad \text{C}-\text{H}$$

At this stage, each hydrogen atom has satisfied its valency of 1.

steps.title: Satisfy carbon's valency
steps.content:
Now we look at the carbon atoms. Each carbon needs a total of **4 bonds**, but currently only has **1 bond** (to a hydrogen). 

To satisfy the remaining valency, each carbon atom needs **3 more bonds**. Since the only available bonding partner for each carbon is the other carbon atom, they must share three pairs of electrons, forming a **triple bond**:

$$\text{H}-\text{C} \equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$$

steps.title: Final verification
steps.content:
Let's check the final structure:
*   Each **H** has 1 bond (satisfies valency).
*   Each **C** has 4 bonds (1 single + 1 triple = 4; satisfies valency/octet).
*   There are no remaining valence electrons, so there are no lone pairs in this molecule.

The final Lewis structure is:
$$\text{H}-\text{C} \equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$$

highlights.text: H-C≡C-H

parts.content: State the hybridization of each carbon atom in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the hybridization: $sp$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the molecule's structure
steps.content: To determine the hybridization of carbon in acetylene (ethyne), $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, we first need to visualize its bonding arrangement. Each carbon atom is bonded to one hydrogen atom and to the other carbon atom.

highlights.text: acetylene, C_2H_2

steps.title: Draw the Lewis structure
steps.content:
Using the rules for covalent bonding, we find that the carbons share three pairs of electrons to form a triple bond, ensuring each carbon has a full octet. The Lewis structure is:

$$\text{H}-\text{C} \equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$$

steps.title: Count electron domains
steps.content:
In the **Electron Domain Approach**, we count the number of regions of electron density (bonds or lone pairs) around the central atom. For one carbon atom in ethyne:
1.  There is one single bond to a hydrogen atom (**1 domain**).
2.  There is one triple bond to the other carbon atom (**1 domain**).

$$\text{Total electron domains} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

steps.title: Determine the hybridization
steps.content:
The number of electron domains directly corresponds to the type of hybridization required to accommodate them:
- **2 domains**: $sp$ hybridization (linear)
- **3 domains**: $sp^2$ hybridization (trigonal planar)
- **4 domains**: $sp^3$ hybridization (tetrahedral)

Since each carbon has 2 domains, it is **$sp$ hybridized**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The hybridization of each carbon atom in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ is **$sp$**.

highlights.text: hybridization of each carbon atom

steps.title: Draw the Lewis structure
steps.content:
To understand the hybridization, we first look at how the atoms are bonded. In acetylene, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, each carbon atom is bonded to one hydrogen atom and the other carbon atom. To satisfy the octet rule for carbon, there must be a triple bond between the two carbons:

$$\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$$

steps.title: Determine the molecular geometry
steps.content:
Using VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion), we count the electron domains around one carbon atom. 

Each carbon atom has **two electron domains**:
1. The single bond to the hydrogen atom.
2. The triple bond to the other carbon atom.

Two electron domains arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion, resulting in a **linear molecular geometry** with a bond angle of $180^\circ$.

steps.title: Identify the hybridization
steps.content:
In the IB syllabus, specific molecular geometries correspond to specific hybridization states. 

A **linear geometry** with a **180-degree bond angle** is the characteristic feature of **$sp$ hybridization**. This occurs when one $s$ orbital and one $p$ orbital mix to form two equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented in opposite directions.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since both carbon atoms in acetylene have the same linear bonding environment, both are $sp$ hybridized.

**Final Answer:** 
$sp$ hybridization

steps.title: Identify the bond types
steps.content:
To determine the hybridization using the **Multiple Bond Recognition** approach, we first look at the Lewis structure of acetylene, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$:

$$\text{H}-\text{C} \equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$$

Notice that there is a **triple bond** between the two carbon atoms. This is a key piece of information from the question.

highlights.text: acetylene, C2H2

steps.title: Analyze pi bond requirements
steps.content:
In any multiple bond, the first bond is a sigma ($\sigma$) bond, and any additional bonds are pi ($\pi$) bonds. 

For a triple bond:
- $1$ bond is a $\sigma$ bond.
- $2$ bonds are $\pi$ bonds.

According to the principles of orbital overlap (which you can review in **Topic S2.2.15: Sigma and Pi Bonds**), each $\pi$ bond requires one **unhybridized** p-orbital. Therefore, each carbon atom must keep two p-orbitals "pure" and unhybridized to form these two $\pi$ bonds.

steps.title: Determine the hybridization
steps.content:
Carbon has a total of four valence orbitals: one $s$ and three $p$ orbitals. 

If we subtract the two $p$ orbitals required for the triple bond's $\pi$ components, we are left with:
- $1$ $s$-orbital
- $1$ $p$-orbital

These remaining two orbitals mix (hybridize) to form two identical hybrid orbitals. Mixing one $s$ and one $p$ results in **$sp$ hybridization**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since each carbon atom in acetylene is involved in a triple bond and requires two unhybridized $p$ orbitals, the hybridization for both carbon atoms is $sp$. 

This results in a linear geometry with a bond angle of $180^\circ$.

parts.content: Identify and explain the types of bonds ($\sigma$ and $\pi$) in the molecule.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the $\sigma$ bond as being formed by the head-on overlap of orbitals (such as $sp$ hybrid orbitals) <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify the $\pi$ bonds as being formed by the side-on overlap of unhybridized $p$ orbitals <marks>M1</marks>
- State that the triple bond in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ consists of one $\sigma$ bond and two $\pi$ bonds <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the molecule's structure
steps.content:
To understand the bonding in acetylene, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, we first look at its structure. Each carbon atom is bonded to one hydrogen and one other carbon atom in a linear arrangement:

$$\text{H}-\text{C} \equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$$

In this molecule, the carbon atoms are **sp hybridized**. This leaves two unhybridized $p$ orbitals on each carbon atom available for multiple bonding. This topic is covered in **Structure 2.2.15: Sigma and Pi Bonds** and **2.2.16: Hybridization and Bonding**.

highlights.text: bonding in acetylene, C2H2

steps.title: Explain sigma (́σ) bond formation
steps.content:
A **sigma ($\sigma$) bond** is formed by the **head-on overlap** of atomic or hybrid orbitals. 

In acetylene, $\sigma$ bonds exist between:
1. The $sp$ hybrid orbital of one carbon and the $1s$ orbital of hydrogen.
2. The $sp$ hybrid orbitals of the two carbon atoms overlapping along the internuclear axis.

According to the markscheme, identifying the $\sigma$ bond as a result of head-on overlap (typically using $sp$ hybrid orbitals here) earns the first mark.

steps.title: Explain pi (π) bond formation
steps.content:
A **pi ($\pi$) bond** is formed by the **side-on overlap** of unhybridized $p$ orbitals. 

In $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$, each carbon has two $p$ orbitals that did not participate in hybridization. These orbitals are oriented perpendicular to the $\sigma$ bond axis. They overlap side-on with the corresponding $p$ orbitals on the adjacent carbon atom to form two $\pi$ bonds. This earns the second mark.

steps.title: Summarize the triple bond
steps.content:
In conclusion, the triple bond between the carbon atoms in acetylene is composed of:
- **One $\sigma$ bond** (from head-on overlap)
- **Two $\pi$ bonds** (from side-on overlap)

Stating this specific composition for the $\text{C} \equiv \text{C}$ triple bond provides the final mark for the question.

steps.title: Determine Carbon Hybridization
steps.content:
To understand the bonding in acetylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$), we first look at the hybridization of the carbon atoms. In this linear molecule, each carbon atom is bonded to two other atoms (one hydrogen and one carbon), which corresponds to two electron domains. 

This requires **$sp$ hybridization**, where one $s$ orbital and one $p$ orbital mix to form two $sp$ hybrid orbitals, leaving two unhybridized $p$ orbitals on each carbon atom.

highlights.text: acetylene, \text{C}_2\text{H}_2,

steps.title: Explain the Sigma Bond
steps.content:
A **sigma ($\sigma$) bond** is formed by the **head-on overlap** of atomic or hybrid orbitals along the axis connecting the nuclei. 

In acetylene, the C-C $\sigma$ bond is formed by the head-on overlap of $sp$ hybrid orbitals from each carbon atom. Additionally, each carbon forms a $\sigma$ bond with a hydrogen atom through the overlap of an $sp$ hybrid orbital and the hydrogen $1s$ orbital.

highlights.text: Identify and explain the types of bonds (\sigma and \pi)

steps.title: Explain the Pi Bonds
steps.content:
A **pi ($\pi$) bond** is formed by the **side-on overlap** of **unhybridized $p$ orbitals**. 

Because each carbon in acetylene has two unhybridized $p$ orbitals that are perpendicular to each other and the sigma bond axis, they overlap side-on to form **two $\pi$ bonds**. These bonds occupy the space above, below, and to the sides of the internuclear axis.

steps.title: Conclusion: The Triple Bond
steps.content:
Combining these overlaps, we can conclude that the triple bond between the carbon atoms in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ consists of **one $\sigma$ bond** and **two $\pi$ bonds**.

This provides the three bonds necessary to satisfy the octet rule for carbon while maintaining a linear geometry.

parts.content: Compare the bond lengths and strengths of the triple bond in $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$ to a double bond in ethene.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the triple bond is shorter and stronger than the double bond <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify bond orders
steps.content:
To compare these bonds, we first identify the type of carbon-carbon bond in each molecule as described in **Topic S2.2.2 (Single, double and triple bonds)**:

*   **Acetylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$):** Contains a carbon-carbon **triple bond**, which has a **bond order of 3**.
*   **Ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$):** Contains a carbon-carbon **double bond**, which has a **bond order of 2**.

highlights.text: triple bond in C2H2
double bond in ethene

steps.title: Apply Bond Order principles
steps.content:
In chemistry, the **bond order** represents the number of chemical bonds between a pair of atoms. There is a predictable relationship between bond order, length, and strength:

1.  **Bond Length:** As bond order increases, more electrons are shared between the nuclei. This increases the electrostatic attraction, pulling the nuclei closer together and making the bond **shorter**.
2.  **Bond Strength:** A higher bond order requires more energy to overcome the stronger electrostatic attractions, making the bond **stronger**.

steps.title: Compare length and strength
steps.content:
Since the triple bond in acetylene ($BO = 3$) has a higher bond order than the double bond in ethene ($BO = 2$):

*   The triple bond is **shorter**.
*   The triple bond is **stronger**.

This single comparison is what earns the mark according to the markscheme.

highlights.text: State that the triple bond is shorter and stronger than the double bond

steps.title: Identify the bond types
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to identify the specific carbon-carbon bonds in both molecules. 

*   Acetylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$) contains a **triple bond** ($$\text{C} \equiv \text{C}$$).
*   Ethene (ethylene) contains a **double bond** ($$\text{C} = \text{C}$$).

This comparison is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

highlights.text: triple bond in C2H2

steps.title: Analyze bond length
steps.content:
We look at **Section 10** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet to find the bond lengths:

*   $$\text{C} = \text{C}$$ bond length: $$134\text{ pm}$$
*   $$\text{C} \equiv \text{C}$$ bond length: $$120\text{ pm}$$

Comparing these values, the triple bond in acetylene is **shorter** than the double bond in ethene because the increased electron density between the nuclei pulls them closer together.

highlights.text: bond lengths

steps.title: Analyze bond strength
steps.content:
Next, we check **Section 11** for the average bond enthalpies, which represent bond strength:

*   $$\text{C} = \text{C}$$ bond enthalpy: $$614\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$
*   $$\text{C} \equiv \text{C}$$ bond enthalpy: $$839\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

A higher bond enthalpy means more energy is required to break the bond, indicating the triple bond is **stronger**.

highlights.text: strengths

steps.title: State final comparison
steps.content:
Based on the data analysis, the carbon-carbon triple bond in acetylene is **shorter and stronger** than the carbon-carbon double bond in ethene.

To earn the mark, you must state both characteristics clearly.

highlights.text: State that the triple bond is shorter and stronger than the double bond
