questions.specification:
The table below shows the experimental bond angles of various molecules, along with the predicted bond angle according to VSEPR theory.

| Molecule | Molecular Shape | Predicted Bond Angle ($^\circ$) | Experimental Bond Angle ($^\circ$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\mathrm{CH}_4$ | Tetrahedral | 109.5 | 109.5 |
| $\mathrm{NH}_3$ | Trigonal Pyramidal | 107 | 106.8 |
| $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ | Bent | 104.5 | 104.5 |
| $\mathrm{BF}_3$ | Trigonal Planar | 120 | 120 |
| $\mathrm{SO}_2$ | Bent | 120 | 119.3 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.4 VSEPR theory
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.4 VSEPR theory
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on VSEPR predictions vs experimental bond angles and deviations caused by lone pairs/electron-pair repulsions (NH3, H2O) and geometry effects in SO2.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.11 Resonance structures (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.11 Resonance structures (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explaining why SO2 experimental angle differs from the ideal VSEPR value commonly involves resonance/delocalization affecting electron density and bond character, which can alter bond angles.

parts.content: Identify and explain why the predicted bond angles for $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ differ from a perfect tetrahedral angle of $109.5^\circ$.
parts.markscheme:
* Both molecules have tetrahedral electron geometry but different bond angles due to lone pair-bond pair repulsion <marks>1 mark</marks>
* $\mathrm{NH}_3$ has one lone pair which pushes bonding pairs closer together, resulting in bond angle of $107^\circ$ (smaller than $109.5^\circ$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ has two lone pairs which causes even stronger repulsion, resulting in even smaller bond angle of $104.5^\circ$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Note:** Award marks for correct bond angles even if explanation is incomplete

steps.title: Identify electron domain geometry
steps.content:
To understand the bond angles, we first look at the number of electron domains around the central atom. $\mathrm{CH}_4$, $\mathrm{NH}_3$, and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ all have four electron domains, giving them a **tetrahedral electron geometry**.

In a perfect tetrahedron like $\mathrm{CH}_4$ (where all domains are bonding pairs), the bond angle is exactly $109.5^\circ$ because the repulsion between the four bonding pairs is equal.

highlights.text: Tetrahedral

steps.title: Apply the VSEPR hierarchy
steps.content:
The **VSEPR Repulsion Hierarchy** states that different types of electron pairs exert different amounts of repulsive force. Lone pairs are held closer to the central nucleus and occupy more space than bonding pairs. 

The order of repulsion strength is:
$$\text{Lone Pair-Lone Pair (LP-LP)} > \text{Lone Pair-Bonding Pair (LP-BP)} > \text{Bonding Pair-Bonding Pair (BP-BP)}$$

When a lone pair is present, it "pushes" the bonding pairs closer to each other, reducing the bond angle.

steps.title: Analyze ammonia (NH3)
steps.content:
$\mathrm{NH}_3$ has **one lone pair** and three bonding pairs. 

Because the repulsion between the lone pair and the bonding pairs (LP-BP) is stronger than the repulsion between the bonding pairs themselves (BP-BP), the H-N-H bonds are squeezed together. This reduces the angle from the ideal $109.5^\circ$ to approximately $107^\circ$.

highlights.text: Trigonal Pyramidal

steps.title: Analyze water (H2O)
steps.content:
$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ has **two lone pairs** and two bonding pairs.

Following the hierarchy, the LP-LP repulsion is the strongest of all. These two lone pairs take up even more space than the single lone pair in $\mathrm{NH}_3$, resulting in an even greater compression of the bonding pairs. This leads to a further reduction in the bond angle to $104.5^\circ$.

highlights.text: Bent

steps.title: Summarize the findings
steps.content:
In summary, while all three molecules share the same tetrahedral electron geometry, the presence of lone pairs increases repulsion against the bonding pairs. 

* **$\mathrm{CH}_4$ (0 lone pairs):** $109.5^\circ$
* **$\mathrm{NH}_3$ (1 lone pair):** $107^\circ$ (compressed by $\approx 2.5^\circ$)
* **$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ (2 lone pairs):** $104.5^\circ$ (compressed by a further $\approx 2.5^\circ$)

steps.title: Establish the Common Baseline
steps.content:
To understand why these angles differ, we first look at the total number of electron domains around the central atoms of $\mathrm{CH}_4$, $\mathrm{NH}_3$, and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$. This is a core concept in **Topic S2.2.4: VSEPR theory**.

All three molecules have **4 electron domains**, which gives them a **tetrahedral electron geometry**. In a perfect tetrahedron where all domains are identical (like in $\mathrm{CH}_4$), the bond angle is exactly $109.5^\circ$.

highlights.text: Both molecules have tetrahedral electron geometry but different bond angles due to lone pair-bond pair repulsion

steps.title: Analyze Ammonia (NH3)
steps.content:
In $\mathrm{NH}_3$, the nitrogen atom has 3 bonding pairs and **1 lone pair**. 

According to VSEPR theory, lone pairs are more "space-filling" than bonding pairs because they are attracted to only one nucleus. This results in **lone pair-bonding pair (LP-BP) repulsion** being stronger than bonding pair-bonding pair (BP-BP) repulsion. This extra repulsion "pushes" the N-H bonds closer together, reducing the angle from $109.5^\circ$ to approximately $107^\circ$.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{NH}_3$ has one lone pair which pushes bonding pairs closer together, resulting in bond angle of $107^\circ$ (smaller than $109.5^\circ$)

steps.title: Analyze Water (H2O)
steps.content:
In $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$, the oxygen atom has 2 bonding pairs and **2 lone pairs**. 

Because there are now two lone pairs, the repulsion is even more significant. Not only do we have LP-BP repulsion, but we also have **lone pair-lone pair (LP-LP) repulsion**, which is the strongest type of repulsion. This forces the O-H bonds even closer together than in ammonia, resulting in an even smaller experimental bond angle of $104.5^\circ$.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ has two lone pairs which causes even stronger repulsion, resulting in even smaller bond angle of $104.5^\circ$

steps.title: Summarize the Comparative Trend
steps.content:
By comparing these molecules, we see a clear trend: as the number of lone pairs increases, the bond angle decreases. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Molecule} & : \mathrm{CH}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} \\ \text{Lone Pairs} & : 0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2 \\ \text{Bond Angle} & : 109.5^\circ \rightarrow 107^\circ \rightarrow 104.5^\circ \end{aligned}$$

In the IB, you can often remember that each lone pair typically reduces the ideal tetrahedral angle by about $2.5^\circ$.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the ammonia angle from the methane angle, then the water angle from the ammonia angle.

desmos.instructions: Enter the subtractions to see the consistent decrease.

calculator.description: Verify the decrease in bond angle per lone pair.

parts.content: Explain the difference in predicted and experimental bond angles for $\mathrm{SO}_2$.
parts.markscheme:
* VSEPR theory predicts bent structure with bond angle of approximately $120^\circ$ due to three electron domains around sulfur <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Actual bond angle is $119.3^\circ$ due to lone pair-bond pair repulsion being greater than bond pair-bond pair repulsion, causing slight reduction from predicted angle <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Determine electron domains
steps.content:
To explain the geometry of $\mathrm{SO}_2$, we first count the electron domains around the central sulfur atom. A domain can be a single bond, a double bond, or a lone pair. Sulfur has:
- Two bonding domains (double bonds to the oxygen atoms)
- One non-bonding domain (a lone pair)

This gives a total of **three electron domains**.

highlights.text: SO_2

steps.title: Justify predicted bond angle
steps.content: According to VSEPR theory, three electron domains arrange themselves in a **trigonal planar** electron geometry to minimize repulsion. The ideal angle for a perfect trigonal planar arrangement is exactly $120^\circ$.

highlights.text: three electron domains around sulfur

steps.title: Apply repulsion principles
steps.content:
VSEPR theory states that electron pairs repel each other with different strengths. Specifically, **lone pair–bond pair (LP-BP) repulsion** is stronger than **bond pair–bond pair (BP-BP) repulsion**. 

In $\mathrm{SO}_2$, the lone pair on the sulfur atom pushes the sulfur-oxygen bonding pairs closer together than they would be in a perfectly symmetrical trigonal planar molecule.

highlights.text: lone pair-bond pair repulsion being greater than bond pair-bond pair repulsion

steps.title: Conclude the angle difference
steps.content: Because of this increased repulsion from the lone pair, the bonding pairs are squeezed together, resulting in a bond angle slightly less than the predicted $120^\circ$. This accounts for the experimental value of $119.3^\circ$.

parts.content: Calculate the percentage difference between the predicted and experimental bond angles of $\mathrm{SO}_2$.
parts.markscheme:
* Correct formula used: $\text{Percentage difference} = \frac{(\text{Predicted} - \text{Experimental})}{\text{Predicted}} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Correct substitution and answer: $\frac{(120 - 119.3)}{120} \times 100 = 0.58\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Exam Tip:** Remember to include the % symbol in your final answer

steps.title: Identify the values
steps.content:
To calculate the percentage difference, we first need to extract the specific data for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ from the table provided. 

*   **Predicted Bond Angle:** $120^\circ$
*   **Experimental Bond Angle:** $119.3^\circ$

steps.title: State the formula
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, the percentage difference (often used to compare experimental results to theoretical/predicted models) is calculated using the following formula:

$$\text{Percentage difference} = \frac{|\text{Predicted} - \text{Experimental}|}{\text{Predicted}} \times 100$$

steps.title: Calculate the difference
steps.content:
Substitute the values for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ into the formula:

$$\text{Percentage difference} = \frac{(120 - 119.3)}{120} \times 100$$

First, find the absolute difference:
$$120 - 119.3 = 0.7$$

Then, divide by the predicted value and multiply by 100:
$$\frac{0.7}{120} \times 100 \approx 0.5833...$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (120 - 119.3) / 120 * 100 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage difference for SO2

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
Rounding to a reasonable number of decimal places (as shown in the markscheme), we get:

**Percentage difference = 0.58%**

**Mark distribution:**
*   **1 mark** for showing the correct formula or substitution.
*   **1 mark** for the correct final answer with the **% symbol**.

steps.title: Identify the bond angles
steps.content:
First, we need to extract the correct values for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ from the table provided in the question. 

*   **Predicted Bond Angle:** $120^\circ$
*   **Experimental Bond Angle:** $119.3^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate the percentage ratio
steps.content:
In the **Complementary Percentage Approach**, we first find what percentage the experimental value is of the predicted value. 

Divide the experimental angle by the predicted angle and multiply by $100$:

$$\text{Percentage of Predicted} = \frac{119.3}{120} \times 100$$

steps.title: Use your calculator
steps.content: Using a calculator for the division and multiplication, we find the ratio as a percentage.

ti84.instructions: Enter 119.3 divided by 120, then multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage ratio of the experimental value to the predicted value.

steps.title: Find the percentage difference
steps.content:
The percentage difference is the amount needed to reach $100\%$. We subtract our previous result from $100\%$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Percentage Difference} &= 100\% - 99.416...\% \\ &= 0.5833...\% \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two decimal places (as shown in the markscheme), we get **$0.58\%$**.

steps.title: Final answer check
steps.content:
The calculation shows a very small difference, which makes sense as $119.3$ is very close to $120$. This small deviation is due to the lone pair on the sulfur atom in $\mathrm{SO}_2$ exerting slightly more repulsion than the bonding pairs, a key concept in **VSEPR theory** (Topic S2.2.4).

Final Answer: $0.58\%$
